text: The video displays a laparoscopic repair procedure for a Morgagni hernia, showcasing the surgical approach and techniques used.
A thirty-two years old male patient presents with two month history of dyspnea and abdominal discomfort.
The patient has no previous history of abdominal operations or major trauma.
CT scan evaluation revealed a large morgani hernia containing omenal fat.
The surgeon employs a three-port technique for the laparoscopic repair of the Morgagni hernia, setting up the surgical field for the procedure.

During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon explores the abdomen and identifies an anterior diaphragmatic defect. The herniated greater omentum is grasped with forceps and repositioned entirely into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon dissects and excises the hernia sac from the thoracic region, removing it from the surrounding tissue.
Many technical aspects are still under debate.
Excision of the sac is suggested in order to reduce recurrence rate if it can be dissected away from the mediastinal structures easily.
The advantage of excising the hernia sac is to avoid a potentially empty space left behind in the thoracic cavity.
Life-threatening complications, massive pneumomediastinum and damage to the pericardium or mediastinal structures were reported during the dissection of the sac.
Following the excision of the hernia sac, the surgeon visually assesses the dimensions of the defect in the thoracic region.
The surgeon removes the excised hernia sac from the surgical field using an endobag.
The method for closure of the defect is variable.
The surgeon repairs the hernia defect using interrupted non-absorbable sutures, closing the visible opening in the tissue.
The surgeon places X-shaped stitches and U-shaped mattress interrupted sutures to secure the repaired hernia defect.
The use of absorbable suture may be associated with recurrence.
Another controversial issue is whether or not to use a prosthetic patch.
It has been hypothesized that simple closure could be the cause of a high recurrence rate because of the high tension.
The use of a patch was suggested in order to decrease recurrence rate.
The surgeon performs a mesh repair technique using a tailored composite polypropylene mesh, securing it in place with absorbable fixation devices.
The use of tacking devices can potentially cause damage to deep structures
No drains were placed after the procedure.
The patient made an eventful recovery and was discharged on the fifth postoperative day.
He was symptom free at five years follow up.
The control CT scan showed no hernia recurrence.
